title: Can a long-distance relationship stay equal?
summary: Argue whether two people separated by distance and time zones can keep their relationship balanced, or whether distance always tilts it.
topic: Whether a long-distance relationship can remain equal
prompt: Write a 450–600-word essay arguing whether a relationship conducted across distance and time zones can stay genuinely equal. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, passive voice, and hedging.
hintStarters: What distance quietly redistributes is not affection but…
Granted, there are couples whose bond seems to deepen rather than fray when…
It is the one who waits up until 2 a.m. to call, not the one who simply calls, who…
Equality in a relationship is less about feelings than about…

hintVocab.meaningVi: gánh nặng của việc liên lạc
cảm thấy bị coi là điều hiển nhiên
tính toán hơn thua / ghi nhớ ai làm gì

sampleText:
Whenever people talk about the difficulty of loving someone far away, they describe missing them — the empty side of the bed, the meals eaten alone. That is real, but it is not the hardest part. What distance quietly redistributes is not affection but labour: the daily, unglamorous work of keeping a relationship alive falls unevenly, and the question of whether love can remain equal turns on who carries that weight.

Consider two people separated by eight time zones, one in Lagos and one in Toronto. For them to speak at all, someone must sacrifice sleep. If it is always the same person who stays up until two in the morning, an imbalance is being created that no amount of affection erases. It is the one who waits up to call, not the one who merely answers, who is paying the higher price — and prices that are paid silently tend to be the ones that accumulate. A second asymmetry is financial: visits cost money and leave, and when one partner earns far more, or holds a passport that crosses borders easily, the burden of making the trip is rarely shared down the middle.

The deeper point is that equality in a relationship is less about feelings than about reciprocity — about whether the effort flows both ways over time. Distance does not change this principle; it simply makes the accounting harder to ignore. When you live in the same city, an uneven week is absorbed by a hundred small exchanges. When you live apart, every call must be scheduled, every visit planned, and the imbalance is laid bare in a way that proximity would have softened.

Granted, there are couples whose bond seems to deepen rather than fray when stretched across a map, and it would be dishonest to pretend otherwise. Some people communicate more honestly in writing than in person; some find that scarcity sharpens attention rather than dulling it. For these couples, distance is not a deficit to be endured but a discipline that strips a relationship down to its essentials. The objection is well taken: distance is not automatically corrosive.

Even so, what these flourishing couples share is not the absence of imbalance but a deliberate effort to correct it. They take turns being the one to call. They name the cost of the late nights out loud rather than letting resentment build. In other words, their equality is not a happy accident of distance but something that is actively maintained against it. Where that maintenance is neglected, the same patterns reassert themselves: one person carries the relationship, and the other, often without noticing, is carried.

So can a long-distance relationship stay equal? It can — but not on its own, and not for free. Equality at a distance has to be built, audited, and rebuilt, because the natural tendency of separation is to push the weight onto whoever is willing to bear it. The couples who manage it are not the ones who love hardest; they are the ones who refuse to let love disguise an unequal ledger as devotion.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft and it-cleft to reassign blame and emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What distance quietly redistributes is not affection but labour' (what-cleft) và 'It is the one who waits up to call, not the one who merely answers, who is paying the higher price' (it-cleft) dùng cấu trúc chẻ câu để loại trừ cách hiểu sai (tình cảm / người bắt máy) và làm nổi bật cách hiểu đúng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What distance quietly redistributes is not affection but labour' (what-cleft) and 'It is the one who waits up to call, not the one who merely answers, who is paying the higher price' (it-cleft) use cleft structures to rule out a misreading (affection / the answerer) and spotlight the correct one.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to foreground the imbalance, not the doer
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'an imbalance is being created', 'the imbalance is laid bare', và 'the other … is carried' dùng bị động để giữ trọng tâm vào sự mất cân bằng như một quá trình, chứ không phải chỉ ra cá nhân nào gây ra — phù hợp với lập luận về cấu trúc hơn là đổ lỗi.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'an imbalance is being created', 'the imbalance is laid bare', and 'the other … is carried' use the passive to keep focus on the imbalance as a process rather than naming a culprit — fitting for an argument about structure rather than blame.

sampleTranslationVi:
Mỗi khi người ta nói về cái khó của việc yêu một người ở xa, họ mô tả nỗi nhớ — nửa giường trống trải, những bữa ăn một mình. Điều đó là thật, nhưng nó không phải phần khó nhất. Cái mà khoảng cách lặng lẽ phân phối lại không phải là tình cảm mà là lao động: công việc thường nhật, chẳng hào nhoáng của việc giữ một mối quan hệ sống động rơi xuống không đồng đều, và câu hỏi liệu tình yêu có thể vẫn ngang bằng hay không xoay quanh việc ai gánh vác sức nặng đó.

Hãy xét đến hai người cách nhau tám múi giờ, một ở Lagos và một ở Toronto. Để họ có thể nói chuyện được với nhau, ai đó phải hy sinh giấc ngủ. Nếu luôn là cùng một người thức đến hai giờ sáng, thì một sự mất cân bằng đang được tạo ra mà không lượng tình cảm nào xóa được. Chính người thức để gọi, chứ không phải người chỉ bắt máy, mới là người đang trả cái giá cao hơn — và những cái giá được trả trong im lặng có xu hướng là những cái tích tụ dần. Một sự bất đối xứng thứ hai là về tài chính: các chuyến thăm tốn tiền và tốn ngày phép, và khi một người kiếm được nhiều hơn hẳn, hoặc giữ một tấm hộ chiếu dễ dàng qua biên giới, thì gánh nặng của việc thực hiện chuyến đi hiếm khi được chia đôi.

Điểm sâu xa hơn là sự bình đẳng trong một mối quan hệ ít liên quan đến cảm xúc mà liên quan đến sự có qua có lại nhiều hơn — về việc liệu công sức có chảy theo cả hai chiều qua thời gian hay không. Khoảng cách không thay đổi nguyên tắc này; nó chỉ làm cho việc tính toán khó mà phớt lờ hơn. Khi bạn sống cùng một thành phố, một tuần lệch lạc được hấp thụ bởi cả trăm trao đổi nhỏ. Khi bạn sống xa nhau, mỗi cuộc gọi phải được lên lịch, mỗi chuyến thăm phải được lên kế hoạch, và sự mất cân bằng bị phơi bày theo cách mà sự gần gũi lẽ ra đã làm dịu đi.

Công nhận, có những cặp đôi mà mối dây liên kết dường như sâu sắc hơn thay vì rạn nứt khi bị kéo căng qua bản đồ, và thật không trung thực nếu giả vờ điều ngược lại. Một số người giao tiếp trung thực hơn qua chữ viết so với khi gặp mặt; một số người thấy rằng sự khan hiếm làm sắc bén sự chú ý thay vì làm nó cùn đi. Đối với những cặp đôi này, khoảng cách không phải một sự thiếu hụt phải chịu đựng mà là một kỷ luật lột bỏ một mối quan hệ xuống còn những điều cốt yếu. Phản đối này được tiếp nhận xác đáng: khoảng cách không tự động mang tính ăn mòn.

Dẫu vậy, điều mà những cặp đôi đang nở rộ này chia sẻ không phải là sự vắng mặt của bất cân bằng mà là một nỗ lực có chủ ý để sửa chữa nó. Họ thay phiên nhau là người gọi điện. Họ gọi tên cái giá của những đêm thức khuya thành lời thay vì để sự oán giận tích tụ. Nói cách khác, sự bình đẳng của họ không phải một tình cờ may mắn của khoảng cách mà là điều được duy trì một cách chủ động chống lại nó. Nơi việc duy trì đó bị bỏ bê, những khuôn mẫu cũ tự khẳng định lại: một người gánh vác mối quan hệ, và người kia, thường không nhận ra, đang được gánh đỡ.

Vậy một mối quan hệ yêu xa có thể vẫn bình đẳng không? Có thể — nhưng không phải tự nó, và không phải miễn phí. Sự bình đẳng từ xa phải được xây dựng, rà soát và xây dựng lại, bởi vì xu hướng tự nhiên của sự chia cách là đẩy sức nặng lên bất cứ ai sẵn lòng gánh nó. Những cặp đôi xoay xở được không phải là những người yêu mạnh mẽ nhất; họ là những người từ chối để tình yêu ngụy trang một bảng cân đối bất bình đẳng thành sự tận tụy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to redirect attention from affection to labour?
options: What distance quietly redistributes is not affection but labour.
Distance redistributes what is not affection quietly but the labour.
Affection is what distance redistributes quietly but not the labour.
Redistributing affection is what distance does quietly but labour not.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What … redistributes is not X but Y' is a what-cleft delaying the key term for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is not X but Y'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to single out who pays more?
options: The one who waits up to call pays the higher price, which is the answerer not.
It is the one who waits up to call, not the one who merely answers, who is paying the higher price.
Paying the higher price is the one who answers merely not waits up to call.
Who pays the higher price is the answerer it is not the one waits up to call.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, who …' is an it-cleft that contrasts the caller with the answerer.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is X, not Y, who …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to foreground the imbalance?
options: Someone is creating an imbalance that affection cannot erase by them.
An imbalance is being created that no amount of affection erases.
Creating an imbalance is what affection cannot being erased does.
The imbalance creating no affection erases that being done.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'An imbalance is being created' uses the present continuous passive to keep focus on the imbalance.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts 'an imbalance' first with 'is being + past participle'?

options: A feeling of intense affection
A mutual exchange where effort flows both ways
The distance between two cities
The cost of a plane ticket
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'reciprocate' — to return a favour.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces the counter-argument most naturally?
options: Some couples are just better people than others, obviously.
Granted, there are couples whose bond seems to deepen rather than fray when stretched across a map.
Of course long-distance always works out perfectly fine for everyone.
Naturally distance never causes a single problem ever.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' fairly concedes that some couples thrive at a distance.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the opposing case without exaggeration?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option hedges its claim appropriately?
options: Prices that are paid silently are always the ones that accumulate, without exception.
Prices that are paid silently tend to be the ones that accumulate.
Prices paid silently will never accumulate in any relationship at all.
Prices paid silently the ones tend accumulate to be that.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to be' states a general pattern, hedging the claim.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'emotional labour' refer to here?
options: The cost of plane tickets and visas
The effort of managing feelings and sustaining the relationship
A salary earned from a caring profession
The physical work of moving house
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Emotional labour' is the unpaid effort of maintaining relationships and managing feelings.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'labour' as work, applied to feelings.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: Honestly long-distance is just the worst and nobody should bother, full stop.
Equality in a relationship is less about feelings than about reciprocity.
Basically one person gets dumped on and the other just chills, end of story.
It's kind of unfair, you know, but whatever, people make it work somehow.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses precise abstract phrasing; the others slip into casual speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from concession back to the thesis?
options: In conclusion, distance is always fine.
Even so, what these flourishing couples share is not the absence of imbalance but a deliberate effort to correct it.
On the contrary, no couple ever survives distance.
Anyway, that point is not worth discussing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession then advances a refined claim about deliberate effort.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and reasserts the argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to take for granted' mean?
options: To pay someone generously
To assume someone or their effort will always be there, without appreciation
To travel a long distance to visit
To break up by message
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take for granted' means to fail to value something you assume will always be there.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of stopping noticing something because it's always available.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: Equality at a distance has to be built, audited, and rebuilt.
Equality at a distance has building, to audit, and being rebuilt.
Equality at a distance builds, auditing, and to be rebuilt it.
At a distance equality being built and to audit then rebuilds.
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched past participles ('built, audited, … rebuilt') form a clean parallel.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same form?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what distinguishes couples who stay equal at a distance?
options: They love each other more intensely than others.
They actively maintain equality by taking turns and naming costs aloud.
They never travel to see each other.
They earn exactly the same salary.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says their equality 'is actively maintained against' distance, with turn-taking and honesty.
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the fifth paragraph on what flourishing couples do.

options: To move closer together over time
To gradually become less connected without a sudden break
To argue loudly and split up
To travel to different countries for work
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Drift apart' means to slowly lose closeness.
mcQuestions.hint: Picture two boats slowly floating away from each other.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best captures the essay's nuanced conclusion?
options: A long-distance relationship can never be equal.
It can stay equal, but only through effort that is built, audited, and rebuilt.
Distance has no effect on equality at all.
Only rich couples can manage long distance.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion grants the possibility but attaches a strong condition ('not on its own, and not for free').
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that says 'yes, but only if'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'an unequal ledger' suggest figuratively?
options: A broken accounting computer
A record in which one person has given far more than the other
A shared bank account
A list of phone numbers
mcQuestions.explanation: A 'ledger' is an account book; 'unequal' means the give-and-take does not balance.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a balance sheet where the two sides don't match.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an appositive (dash insertion) correctly?
options: It is the one who waits up to call, not the one who merely answers, who pays the higher price.
It is the one — who waits up not the one merely answers — to call who pays the price.
It is the one who pays — waits up to call not who answers — the higher price merely.
Pays the higher price — it is the one who waits — not who answers merely to call up.
mcQuestions.explanation: The contrast 'not the one who merely answers' is set off by commas; the main clause stays grammatical.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps a complete sentence if the inserted contrast is removed?

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a relationship you keep up across distance — a partner, a parent, an old friend. Who usually reaches out first, and who waits? If you tallied a month of contact honestly, would the ledger balance, and what would you change if it didn't?
